entries.title: The Morning Market
entries.editorNote: A short market scene today — listen for the little negotiations that make the verbs useful.
entries.passage: At dawn the square fills with the smell of fresh produce. Every vendor arranges a stall piled high with fruit, and the earliest shoppers arrive to haggle before the best crates are gone. A good bargain, they say, is half the fun; the rest is the talk.

comprehension.prompt: Why do the earliest shoppers arrive before dawn?
options: To haggle for the best crates before they sell out.
Because the market closes at noon.
comprehension.explanation: The passage says they come early to haggle before the best crates are gone.

entries.title: The Long Way Home
entries.editorNote: An evening detour today — the small verbs of a walk that nobody is timing.
entries.passage: The bus sighs at the curb and she lets it go. There is an errand she has been meaning to finish all week, but the light is low and gold, so she decides to linger instead and take the long way home, past the repair shop with its shutters already down. A neighbor is out sweeping his step; they nod at each other and neither stops to talk. Further on, two men argue cheerfully about a football match nobody won. Nothing is settled by the time she reaches her door, and nothing needed to be.

comprehension.prompt: Why does she let the bus go?
options: The evening is pleasant, and she would rather walk slowly than hurry.
The bus was too crowded for her to board.
She was going somewhere the bus does not stop.
comprehension.explanation: She lets it pass because the light is low and gold — she would rather linger than finish her errand.

entries.title: The Bakers Who Work While the City Sleeps
entries.editorNote: Today's edition keeps the hours of the people who make your morning — read it for the verbs of patience.
entries.passage: By half past three the ovens are already warm, and the street outside has the particular quiet that lasts only a few hours. Hùng has worked this shift for eleven years. He says he no longer notices the hour; his body simply wakes. The work is repetitive, though he insists it is never dull. Flour, water, salt, time. He lets the dough rest, then folds it, then lets it rest again. Bread cannot be hurried. It can only be attended to, the way you attend to something that is quietly alive. The younger bakers arrive at four and are, for the first month or so, always a little too fast. They want to knead harder, prove quicker, push the oven hotter. Hùng lets them. The bread teaches them what he cannot. Around five the first batch comes out, and the smell reaches the street before the light does. A taxi driver pulls over without being called; he has stopped here for years and has never once been asked what he wants. By six the shelves are full, the shop is loud, and not one customer will think about the eleven hours standing behind the loaf in their hand. Hùng likes it that way. A good loaf, he says, should look effortless — and that is the whole trick of the trade. The labor disappears into the thing it makes.

comprehension.prompt: What does Hùng mean when he says a good loaf should look effortless?
options: The long, patient work behind it should be invisible to the person eating it.
Baking bread is easier than most people assume it is.
A loaf should be simple in shape, without decoration.
comprehension.explanation: He means the labor disappears into the thing it makes — the customer never sees the eleven hours.
